title: NIS2 Secure System Acquisition and Development Lifecycle
exclusions: Does not confirm that scan findings were remediated, verify the scanning tools cover all relevant vulnerability classes, audit code quality, or replace counsel review of Art.21(2)(e) obligations. Only measures CI/CD security scan coverage per repository.
evidence: Weekly saved search `UC-22.2.15`, exported KPI workbook, and companion panel in the secure engineering dashboard referenced in the NIS2 evidence register.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(e) requires security in systems acquisition, development and maintenance. The UC measures per-repository CI/CD security scan coverage and flags pipelines that deploy without scanning. It cannot confirm that scan findings were actually remediated or that the scanning tools cover all relevant vulnerability classes, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.15: NIS2 Secure System Acquisition and Development Lifecycle.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.15' running on index devops and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture pushes 12 synthetic `jenkins:build` events for `repo-a` with zero `_raw` matches for security tooling words but two events mentioning `deploy to production`; the search returns `repo-a` with `scan_coverage` below 80 and deployments greater than zero.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Fixture sends only events for `repo-b` where each build string includes `sonar` and none mention production deploys; the saved search output is empty for `repo-b` even though build volume is high.

dataSources: `index=devops` `sourcetype=github:audit` (action, repo, actor) and `sourcetype=gitlab:audit` via HEC/webhooks; `index=devops` `sourcetype=jenkins:build` for on-prem pipelines; `index=sast` `sourcetype=sonarqube:issues` (severity, project, key); Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5596](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5596)) REST modular inputs hitting `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions` pulls complement webhook feeds.
app: Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5596](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5596)), Splunk Add-on for Jira ([Splunkbase 1438](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1438)) for change linkage, generic **HTTP Event Collector** for GitLab/Jenkins JSON.
spl:
index=devops sourcetype IN ("github:webhook","gitlab:pipeline","jenkins:build") earliest=-30d
| eval has_security_scan=if(match(lower(_raw),"(?i)sast|sca|snyk|sonar|trivy|semgrep|security.scan|code.scan"), 1, 0)
| eval deployment=if(match(lower(_raw),"(?i)deploy|release|prod|production"), 1, 0)
| stats count as total_builds, sum(has_security_scan) as scanned_builds, sum(deployment) as deployments by repository
| eval scan_coverage=round(100*scanned_builds/total_builds, 1)
| where scan_coverage < 80 OR (deployments > 0 AND scanned_builds = 0)
| sort scan_coverage
| table repository, total_builds, scanned_builds, scan_coverage, deployments
description: Measures, per GitHub/GitLab/Jenkins repository, the share of pipeline runs in the last 30 days that show evidence of a security scan stage and calls out repos that still shipped production keywords without any scanning hits.
value: Executives testing NIS2 development controls get a plain scoreboard of which product teams skip automated checks before code hits production, so security champions know where to spend coaching hours before an audit interview.
implementation: (1) Enable GitHub audit streaming or webhook posts into `index=devops` with `sourcetype=github:webhook`. (2) Mirror GitLab pipeline JSON through HEC token on port **8088** if cloud SaaS cannot install the TA directly. (3) Tag production branches so `deployment` regex matches real releases, not developer chatter. (4) Pair SonarQube issues (`index=sast`) in a sister dashboard for depth. (5) Escalate repos below `80%` coverage to engineering leads in Jira (1438).
visualization: Table of failing repositories, bar chart of scan_coverage, single value fleet average, line trend of coverage over 90 days.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Mono-repo with scan jobs in a different workflow file** — Distinguish: `_raw` never mentions SAST keywords but Sonar runs nightly outside CI. Suppress: lookup `repo_scan_exceptions.csv` documenting alternate job names.
• **Blue/green deploy phrases in logs without customer impact** — Distinguish: developers type "prod-like" in feature branches. Suppress: restrict `deployment=1` to default branch via `where match(branch,"^(main|master|release/)`)` after field extract.
• **Security scanning handled only in release trains quarterly** — Distinguish: policy allows quarterly bulk scans vs per-commit. Suppress: time-bound exception register `sdlc_policy_exceptions.csv` with repo and effective_through.
• **Jenkins shared library hides scan stage text** — Distinguish: `_raw` lacks keywords though library runs scans. Suppress: enrich with `lookup repo_has_external_sonar.csv` and OR that flag into `has_security_scan`.
• **High churn forks or student sandboxes** — Distinguish: repository tagged `classification=personal` in GitHub metadata. Suppress: `where classification!="personal"` after metadata join.
equipmentModels: GitLab CI/CD (sourcetype gitlab:pipeline via webhook or HEC)
Jenkins CI/CD (sourcetype jenkins:build via UF or HTTP plugin)
SonarQube / Snyk / Semgrep / Trivy (SAST/SCA tools referenced in pipeline event text)
Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596, audit log and workflow run ingestion)
Splunk Add-on for Jira (Splunkbase 1438, change/release linkage)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `devops` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(e)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.15: NIS2 Secure System Acquisition and Development Lifecycle." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `devops` under `github:webhook`, `gitlab:pipeline`, `jenkins:build`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Enable GitHub audit streaming or webhook posts into `index=devops` with `sourcetype=github:webhook`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Mirror GitLab pipeline JSON through HEC token on port **8088** if cloud SaaS cannot install the TA directly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Tag production branches so `deployment` regex matches real releases, not developer chatter. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 4:** Pair SonarQube issues (`index=sast`) in a sister dashboard for depth. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Escalate repos below `80%` coverage to engineering leads in Jira. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `scan_coverage < 80`. For "NIS2 Secure System Acquisition and Development Lifecycle," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `80` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by scan_coverage
| stats avg(scan_coverage) as mean, perc50(scan_coverage) as p50, perc90(scan_coverage) as p90, perc99(scan_coverage) as p99, max(scan_coverage) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `80` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `sum()` aggregation over the last 30 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.15: NIS2 Secure System Acquisition and Development Lifecycle]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.15,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=devops sourcetype=github:webhook earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `deployment`, `deployments`, `has_security_scan`, `repository`, `scan_coverage`, `scanned_builds`, `total_builds`. Run:

```spl
index=devops sourcetype=github:webhook earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table deployment, deployments, has_security_scan, repository, scan_coverage, scanned_builds, total_builds
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `scan_coverage`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(scan_coverage) as p50 perc90(scan_coverage) as p90 perc99(scan_coverage) as p99 max(scan_coverage) as hi
```

The current threshold `80` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.15: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Failing Repositories (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Scan Coverage (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value Fleet Average (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Line Trend Of Coverage Over 90 Days (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We check whether the automatic safety checks ran before new software left the factory floor, so risky changes are caught while they are still easy to undo instead of after customers are affected.
